q: Who founded the Gupta Empire and when?
options: Srigupta (c. 240-280 CE), the first Gupta king
explanation: Srigupta (c. 240-280 CE) was the founder of the Gupta Empire. He was the first Gupta king and ruled from Pataliputra (Bihar). His son Ghatotkacha succeeded him. The Gupta era is traditionally dated from Chandragupta I's accession (c. 319-320 CE).

q: Chandragupta I (c. 319-334 CE) is considered the real founder of the Gupta Empire. What was his key achievement?
options: He started the Gupta Era (c. 319-320 CE) and assumed the title 'Maharajadhiraja'
explanation: Chandragupta I (c. 319-334 CE) is considered the real founder. He started the Gupta Era (c. 319-320 CE), assumed the title 'Maharajadhiraja' (King of Great Kings), and married Kumaradevi (a Lichchhavi princess — strengthening the Gupta dynasty). He ruled from Pataliputra (Bihar).

q: Samudragupta (c. 335-380 CE), the greatest Gupta conqueror, is known by which title?
options: 'Napoleon of India' (for his military conquests across India)
explanation: Samudragupta (c. 335-380 CE) is called the 'Napoleon of India' (by historian V.A. Smith) for his military conquests. His Allahabad Pillar inscription (by his court poet Harisena) records his conquests: Aryavarta (direct rule), Dakshinapatha (12 southern kings defeated then released), and frontier kings (paid tribute). He was also a musician (lyrist on coins) and scholar.

q: The Allahabad Pillar Inscription, which records Samudragupta's conquests, was composed by which poet?
explanation: The Allahabad Pillar Inscription (Prayag Prasasti) was composed by Harisena — Samudragupta's court poet, minister, and commander. It records Samudragupta's conquests: Aryavarta (annexed), Dakshinapatha (12 kings defeated, then graciously released — 'grama-grahana-moksha-anugraha'), frontier kings (tribute), and his skills (music, poetry, scholarship).

q: Chandragupta II Vikramaditya (c. 380-415 CE) is famous for defeating which foreign tribe?
options: The Shakas (Western Kshatrapas, c. 409 CE — he defeated Rudrasimha III and annexed Gujarat)
explanation: Chandragupta II Vikramaditya (c. 380-415 CE) defeated the Shakas (Western Kshatrapas) c. 409 CE — killing Rudrasimha III and annexing Gujarat and Malwa. He assumed the title 'Sakari' (enemy of the Shakas) and 'Vikramaditya.' This gave the Guptas access to western ports (Broach, Kalyan, Sopara) and the lucrative trade with the Roman Empire.

q: The 'Navaratna' (Nine Gems) — a council of nine scholars — adorned whose court?
explanation: The Navaratna (Nine Gems) adorned Chandragupta II Vikramaditya's court. The most famous was Kalidasa (poet, author of Shakuntala, Meghaduta, Raghuvamsha). Others included Varahamihira (astronomer), Dhanvantari (physician), Amarasimha (lexicographer), Shanku (architecture), and others. This was the peak of the Gupta 'Golden Age.'

q: Kalidasa, the greatest Sanskrit poet and dramatist, lived during which period?
options: The Gupta period (c. 4th-5th century CE, court poet of Chandragupta II)
explanation: Kalidasa lived during the Gupta period (c. 4th-5th c. CE) and was a Navaratna in Chandragupta II's court. Works: Abhijnanasakuntalam (Shakuntala, drama), Meghaduta (Cloud Messenger, poetry), Raghuvamsha (Raghu dynasty, epic), Kumarasambhava (Birth of Kumara, epic), Ritusamhara (Seasons). He wrote in Sanskrit.

q: The Gupta Empire's capital was at which city (in modern Bihar)?
explanation: The Gupta Empire's capital was Pataliputra (modern Patna, Bihar) — continuing the Mauryan and Shunga tradition. Chandragupta II also maintained Ujjain as a second capital (after conquering Malwa). Pataliputra was the centre of the Gupta 'Golden Age' — administration, culture, and learning (including Nalanda University nearby).

q: Nalanda University, the great Buddhist centre of learning, was founded during the Gupta period by which ruler?
explanation: Nalanda University (near Rajgir, Bihar) was founded by Kumargupta I (c. 427 CE) — son of Chandragupta II. It became one of the world's first residential universities (10,000 students, 2,000 teachers, from China, Korea, Tibet, Central Asia). Subjects: Buddhism, logic, medicine, astronomy. Destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khalji (~1200 CE). UNESCO (2016).

q: Fa-Hien (Faxian), the Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited India during which Gupta king's reign?
explanation: Fa-Hien (Faxian) visited India during Chandragupta II's reign (c. 405-411 CE). He came to collect Buddhist texts and visit holy sites (Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, Sarnath, Kushinagar). His travelogue 'Fo-kwo-ki' (Record of Buddhist Kingdoms) describes Gupta India: peaceful, prosperous, low taxes, people happy, no capital punishment (for first offenses).

q: The Gupta period is called the 'Golden Age' of ancient India. Which of the following is a key reason?
options: All of the above (extraordinary achievements in art, literature, science, mathematics, and religion)
explanation: The Gupta period (c. 320-550 CE) is the 'Golden Age' because of extraordinary achievements in: literature (Kalidasa), science (Aryabhata — mathematics, astronomy), medicine (Sushruta, Charaka), art (Ajanta caves), architecture (temples), metallurgy (Iron Pillar of Delhi), and religion (Hinduism flourished, Buddhism continued). Hinduism was the state religion but Buddhism was tolerated.

q: Aryabhata, the great mathematician and astronomer, lived during the Gupta period. Which of the following is his contribution?
options: All of the above (he wrote the Aryabhatiya, calculated pi, stated the earth rotates on its axis, and explained eclipses)
explanation: Aryabhata (c. 476-550 CE) wrote the Aryabhatiya (c. 499 CE). Contributions: calculated pi (~3.1416), stated the Earth rotates on its axis (explaining day and night), explained lunar and solar eclipses (shadows of Earth/Moon), place-value system, sine table. He was 23 when he wrote the Aryabhatiya. He also wrote the Surya Siddhanta (attributed).

q: The famous Ajanta Caves (Buddhist rock-cut caves with magnificent paintings) were mostly built during which period?
options: The Gupta period (c. 4th-6th century CE, though some earlier)
explanation: The Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra, 29 caves) were mostly built during the Gupta period (c. 4th-6th c. CE) — though some earlier (2nd c. BCE, Shunga/Satavahana). The Gupta-era caves (16-27) have magnificent paintings (frescoes) depicting Jataka tales (previous lives of the Buddha) and Buddhist themes. UNESCO (1983). Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang visited (7th c.).

q: The Iron Pillar of Delhi (Mehrauli) — famous for not rusting — was built during which period?
options: The Gupta period (c. 4th century CE, during Chandragupta II's reign)
explanation: The Iron Pillar of Delhi (Mehrauli) was built during the Gupta period (c. 4th c. CE, Chandragupta II's reign). It is ~7 meters tall, ~6 tons, and famously has NOT rusted in ~1600 years — a testament to Gupta-era metallurgy. It has a Sanskrit inscription praising 'Chandra' (Chandragupta II). Originally at Udayagiri, later moved to Delhi by a Tomara ruler.

q: The Gupta Empire declined due to invasions by which foreign tribe?
options: The Huns (White Huns, c. 5th-6th century CE, from Central Asia)
explanation: The Gupta Empire declined due to invasions by the Huns (White Huns / Hephthalites, from Central Asia) — c. 5th-6th c. CE. Skandagupta (c. 455-467 CE) defeated the Huns but the effort exhausted the treasury. After Skandagupta, the Gupta Empire fragmented. The Huns finally broke the Gupta power under Toramana and Mihirakula (c. 510-530 CE).

q: Skandagupta (c. 455-467 CE), the last great Gupta emperor, is known for which achievement?
options: Defeating the Huns (White Huns) and restoring the Sudarshana Lake
explanation: Skandagupta (c. 455-467 CE) was the last great Gupta emperor. He defeated the Huns (White Huns) — saving India from their invasion temporarily. He also restored the Sudarshana Lake (Junagadh inscription). But the wars exhausted the treasury (gold coins became lighter). After him, the empire fragmented and declined.

q: The Gupta Empire followed which religion as its state religion?
explanation: The Guptas were followers of Brahminical Hinduism — especially Vaishnavism (worship of Vishnu). Chandragupta II took the title 'Paramabhagavata' (supreme devotee of Vishnu). However, the Guptas were tolerant — they patronised Buddhism (founded Nalanda), Jainism, and other sects. This religious tolerance was a Gupta feature.

q: The Gupta administration was characterized by which feature?
options: Decentralized administration with feudatories (samantas) and land grants to Brahmins and temples
A highly centralized bureaucracy like the Mauryas
explanation: Gupta administration was more decentralized than the Mauryan. Key features: feudatories (samantas — subordinate kings who paid tribute and provided military support), land grants (agraharas) to Brahmins and temples (starting the feudal structure), provinces (bhuktis) and districts (vishayas). This decentralization eventually weakened the empire.

q: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang), the Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited India during which period?
explanation: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) visited India during Harsha's reign (c. 630-645 CE) — the post-Gupta period. He spent ~15 years in India, visited Nalanda University (Bihar), and met Harsha. His travelogue 'Si-yu-ki' (Records of the Western Regions) is a crucial source for post-Gupta India. He studied at Nalanda for 5 years.

q: The position of women during the Gupta period was characterized by which feature?
options: Decline in status (no property rights, child marriage, sati practice began, no education)
Women had equal rights to men
explanation: The position of women declined during the Gupta period. Features: no property rights (except stridhana = bridal gifts), child marriage became common, the practice of sati began (first recorded epigraphically at Eran, 510 CE), women were discouraged from education. However, upper-class women (like queens) were educated — Kumaradevi (Chandragupta I's wife) was a Lichchhavi princess.

q: The caste system during the Gupta period was characterized by which feature?
options: Proliferation of sub-castes (jatis) and rigidification of the varna system
explanation: The caste system proliferated and rigidified during the Gupta period. Many new sub-castes (jatis) emerged — due to the mixing of varnas (varna-sankara), the rise of occupational groups, and the absorption of foreigners (Shakas, Kushanas, Huns) into the Kshatriya caste. The number of castes multiplied. Untouchability increased (chandalas, etc.).

q: Bihar's connection with the Gupta Empire is primarily through which fact?
options: All of the above (Pataliputra was the Gupta capital, Nalanda University was founded in Bihar, the Guptas originated from Bihar)
explanation: Bihar was the heartland of the Gupta Empire: (1) Pataliputra (Patna) was the Gupta capital — administrative and cultural centre. (2) Nalanda University was founded by Kumargupta I (c. 427 CE) in Bihar — becoming the world's greatest centre of Buddhist learning. (3) The Guptas likely originated from the Magadha region (Bihar). The 'Golden Age' was centred in Bihar!

q: The Guptas issued which type of coins primarily?
options: Gold coins (dinaras) with portraits of kings and their titles
explanation: The Guptas issued gold coins (dinaras) — the largest gold coinage in ancient India. Coins featured portraits of kings (in various poses: archer, tiger-slayer, lute-player) and their titles. Silver coins (rupaka) were also issued after conquering Gujarat (from the Shakas). The gold coins show the prosperity of the Gupta 'Golden Age.' Later coins became lighter (decline).

q: Which Gupta king is known as the 'musician king' — depicted playing the veena (lute) on his coins?
explanation: Samudragupta (c. 335-380 CE) is depicted playing the veena (lute) on his coins — earning him the title of the 'musician king.' He was a polymath: warrior (Napoleon of India), poet (called 'Kaviraja'), musician (veena player), and scholar. His Allahabad inscription describes his diverse talents.

q: The Gupta Era (starting c. 319-320 CE) is still used in India for which purpose?
options: It is used as the basis for the Indian National Calendar (Saka Era is separate, but the Gupta Era was historically significant for dating)
It is the official calendar of India today
explanation: The Gupta Era (c. 319-320 CE) was historically significant for dating inscriptions, grants, and texts throughout the Gupta and post-Gupta period. While the Saka Era (78 CE) is the official National Calendar of India today, the Gupta Era was widely used for ~300+ years in ancient India. Many Gupta inscriptions are dated in the Gupta Era.
